content:
# Exodus Chapter 20 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~44v–45r (PDF pp. 85–86); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 61–62 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_020_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_020_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_020_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_020_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

## The Decalogue: Preamble (vv. 1–2)

**1** And God spoke [**vayedabber Elohim**] all these words [**et kol-haddevarim ha'elleh**], saying [**le'mor**]:

**2** "I [**anokhi**] am the LORD your God [**YHWH Elohekha**], who brought you out [**asher hotsetikha**] from the land of Egypt [**me'erets Mitsrayim**], from the house of bondage [**mibbetavadim**].

---

## First Commandment: No Other Gods (v. 3)

**3** You shall not have [**lo yihyeh-lekha**] other gods [**elohim acherim**] before My face [**al-panay**].

---

## Second Commandment: No Graven Image (vv. 4–6)

**4** You shall not make [**lo ta'aseh-lekha**] for yourself a carved image [**fesel**] or any likeness [**vekhol-temunah**] of that which is in the heavens [**asher bashamayim**] above [**mimma'al**], or that which is on the earth [**va'asher ba'arets**] beneath [**mittachat**], or that which is in the waters [**va'asher bammayim**] under the earth [**mittachat la'arets**].

**5** You shall not bow down [**lo-tishtachaveh**] to them [**lahem**] and you shall not serve them [**velo to'ovdem**], for I [**ki anokhi**] the LORD your God [**YHWH Elohekha**] am a jealous God [**el qanna**], visiting [**poqed**] the iniquity of the fathers [**avon avot**] upon the sons [**al-banim**], upon the third [**al-shileshim**] and upon the fourth generation [**ve'al-ribbe'im**] of those who hate Me [**leson'ay**],

**6** but showing steadfast love [**ve'oseh chesed**] to thousands [**la'alafim**] of those who love Me [**le'ohavay**] and of those who keep [**uleshomrey**] My commandments [**mitsvotay**].

---

## Third Commandment: The Name (v. 7)

**7** You shall not take up [**lo tissa**] the name [**et-shem**] of the LORD your God [**YHWH Elohekha**] for emptiness [**lashshav**], for the LORD [**ki lo yenaqqeh YHWH**] will not hold guiltless [**lo yenaqqeh**] the one who takes up [**et asher-yissa**] His name [**et-shemo**] for emptiness [**lashshav**].

---

## Fourth Commandment: The Sabbath (vv. 8–11)

**8** Remember [**zakhor**] the Sabbath day [**et-yom hashshabbat**] to keep it holy [**leqaddesho**].

**9** Six days [**sheshet yamim**] you shall labor [**ta'avod**] and do [**ve'asita**] all your work [**kol-melakhtekha**],

**10** but the seventh day [**veyom hashevi'i**] is a Sabbath [**shabbat**] to the LORD your God [**laYHWH Elohekha**]. You shall not do [**lo ta'aseh**] any work [**khol-melakhah**] — you [**attah**], nor your son [**uvinkha**], nor your daughter [**uvittekha**], nor your male servant [**avdekha**], nor your female servant [**va'amatekha**], nor your beast [**uvehemtekha**], nor your sojourner [**vegerekha**] who is within your gates [**asher bish'arekha**].

**11** For in six days [**ki sheshet-yamim**] the LORD made [**asah YHWH**] the heavens [**et-hashshamayim**] and the earth [**ve'et-ha'arets**], the sea [**et-hayyam**] and all that is in them [**ve'et-kol-asher-bam**], and He rested [**vayyanach**] on the seventh day [**bayyom hashevi'i**]. Therefore [**al-ken**] the LORD blessed [**berekh YHWH**] the Sabbath day [**et-yom hashshabbat**] and made it holy [**vayeqaddeshhu**].

---

## Fifth Commandment: Honor Father and Mother (v. 12)

**12** Honor [**kabbed**] your father [**et-avikha**] and your mother [**ve'et-immekha**], so that [**lema'an**] your days may be long [**ya'arikhun yamekha**] upon the land [**al ha'adamah**] which the LORD your God [**asher-YHWH Elohekha**] is giving [**noten**] to you [**lakh**].

---

## Sixth Commandment: Murder (v. 13)

**13** You shall not murder [**lo tirtsach**].

---

## Seventh Commandment: Adultery (v. 14)

**14** You shall not commit adultery [**lo tin'af**].

---

## Eighth Commandment: Theft (v. 15)

**15** You shall not steal [**lo tignov**].

---

## Ninth Commandment: False Witness (v. 16)

**16** You shall not bear [**lo ta'aneh**] against your neighbor [**vere'akha**] a witness of falsehood [**ed shaqer**].

---

## Tenth Commandment: Coveting (v. 17)

**17** You shall not covet [**lo tachmod**] the house of your neighbor [**beyt re'ekha**]. You shall not covet [**lo tachmod**] the wife of your neighbor [**eshet re'ekha**], nor his male servant [**ve'avdo**], nor his female servant [**va'amato**], nor his ox [**veshoro**], nor his donkey [**vachamoro**], nor anything [**vekhol**] that belongs to your neighbor [**asher lere'ekha**].

---

## The People's Response to the Theophany (vv. 18–21)

**18** And all the people [**vekhol-ha'am**] were seeing [**ro'im**] the thunders [**et-haqqolot**] and the torches [**ve'et-hallappidim**] and the sound of the horn [**ve'et qol hashshofar**] and the mountain smoking [**ve'et-hahar ashen**]; and the people saw [**vayyar ha'am**] and they trembled [**vayyanu'u**] and they stood [**vayya'amdu**] at a distance [**merachoq**].

**19** And they said [**vayyo'meru**] to Moses [**el-Mosheh**], "You speak [**dabber-attah**] with us [**immanu**] and we will hear [**venishma'ah**]; but let not God [**ve'al-yedabber immanu**] speak with us [**Elohim**], lest we die [**pen-namut**]."

**20** And Moses said [**vayyomer Mosheh**] to the people [**el-ha'am**], "Do not fear [**al-tira'u**], for God has come [**ki leva'avur nassot etkhem ba ha'Elohim**] in order to test you [**nassot**], and in order that [**uva'avur**] His fear [**yir'ato**] may be [**tihyeh**] upon your faces [**al-penekhem**], so that [**levilti**] you may not sin [**techetau**]."

**21** And the people [**vayyaamod ha'am**] stood [**vayyaamod**] at a distance [**merachoq**], and Moses drew near [**uMosheh niggash**] to the thick darkness [**el-ha'arafel**] where God was [**asher-sham ha'Elohim**].

---

## The Altar Law (vv. 22–26)

**22** And the LORD said [**vayyomer YHWH**] to Moses [**el-Mosheh**], "Thus shall you say [**koh tomar**] to the sons of Israel [**el-beney Yisra'el**]: You yourselves have seen [**attem re'item**] that from the heavens [**ki min-hashshamayim**] I have spoken [**dibbarti**] with you [**immakhem**].

**23** You shall not make [**lo ta'asun**] alongside Me [**itti**] gods of silver [**elohey khesef**], and gods of gold [**velohey zahav**] you shall not make [**lo ta'asu**] for yourselves [**lakhem**].

**24** An altar of earth [**mizbach adamah**] you shall make [**ta'aseh**] for Me [**li**], and you shall sacrifice [**vezavachta**] upon it [**alav**] your burnt offerings [**et-olotekha**] and your peace offerings [**ve'et-shelamekha**], your flocks [**et-tsonekha**] and your herds [**ve'et-beqarekha**]; in every place [**bekhol-hamaqqom**] where I cause My name to be remembered [**asher azkir et-shemi**] I will come [**avo**] to you [**elekha**] and I will bless you [**uverakhttikha**].

**25** And if [**ve'im**] an altar of stones [**mizbach avanim**] you make [**ta'aseh**] for Me [**li**], you shall not build it [**lo-tivneh**] of hewn stones [**ethen gazit**], for your sword [**ki charbkha**] you have swung [**henafta**] over it [**aleha**] and you have profaned it [**vatechalelha**].

**26** And you shall not go up [**velo-ta'aleh**] by steps [**vema'alot**] upon My altar [**al-mizbechi**], so that [**asher**] your nakedness [**ervatekha**] may not be uncovered [**lo-tiggaleh**] upon it [**alav**].

---

## Translator's Notes

### Key Terms

**anokhi** (v. 2) — "I." The preamble opens with the emphatic first-person pronoun anokhi rather than the shorter ani. In biblical Hebrew, anokhi is the solemn, weighty form of self-identification — it carries the full force of the speaker's presence. The formula "anokhi YHWH Elohekha" is the foundational declaration of the entire Decalogue: before any commandment is issued, God identifies Himself. The Decalogue is not a freestanding legal code but a speech act grounded in the identity and prior action of the speaker. The theological priority is clear: "I am" precedes "you shall not." Grace precedes law. The LXX renders this as ego eimi kurios ho theos sou ("I am the Lord your God"), using ego eimi — the same self-identification formula that the Fourth Gospel will place on the lips of Jesus (John 8:58).

**el qanna** (v. 5) — "a jealous God." The adjective qanna (from the root q-n-', "to be jealous, zealous") is applied to God exclusively — it never describes a human being in the Hebrew Bible. This is not jealousy as petty possessiveness but as covenantal exclusivity: the jealousy of a husband for his wife (cf. Num 5:14), the refusal to share what belongs to one alone. The word occurs only six times in the Hebrew Bible (Exod 20:5; 34:14 [twice]; Deut 4:24; 5:9; 6:15), always in the context of idolatry — God is qanna specifically in response to the worship of other gods. The LXX renders it theos zēlōtēs ("a zealous God"), using the same root that produces "Zealot" in the New Testament period.

**zakhor** (v. 8) — "Remember." The Exodus version of the Sabbath commandment uses zakhor ("remember"), while the Deuteronomy 5:12 parallel uses shamor ("keep, guard, observe"). The Talmud (Shevuot 20b) famously declares that both words were spoken "in a single utterance" (bedibbur echad ne'emru) — a theological solution to the textual divergence. The verb zakhor means not merely mental recollection but active commemoration: to remember the Sabbath is to actualize it, to set it apart in practice. The formal equivalence rendering retains "remember" to preserve the distinction from Deuteronomy's "observe." The LXX renders it mnēsthēti ("remember"), an aorist imperative that captures the punctiliar aspect — remember it each time it arrives.

**lo tirtsach** (v. 13) — "You shall not murder." The verb ratsach is carefully distinguished in biblical Hebrew from other words for killing. The verb harag ("to kill, slay") is the general term for taking life, used of both lawful and unlawful killing; the verb hemit ("to put to death") is used for judicial execution; the verb nakah ("to strike, smite") covers lethal blows in various contexts. But ratsach is the specific term for unlawful, unjustified killing — murder, manslaughter, and bloodguilt-incurring homicide. It is the verb used for the act that creates the "blood avenger" (go'el haddam) and that defines the cities of refuge (Num 35:6-34, where ratsach appears twenty times). The commandment does not prohibit all taking of human life (the Torah itself prescribes capital punishment, permits killing in warfare, and allows for self-defense); it prohibits the specific category of unjustified, bloodguilt-creating homicide. The formal equivalence rendering "murder" rather than "kill" preserves this critical semantic boundary. The LXX renders it ou phoneuseis ("you shall not murder"), using phoneuō — the specific term for murder, not the general apokteinō ("to kill").

**lo tachmod** (v. 17) — "You shall not covet." The verb chamad means "to desire, to covet, to take delight in" — it denotes not merely an internal feeling but an acquisitive desire that tends toward action. In biblical usage, chamad often implies reaching out to take what one desires (cf. Josh 7:21, where Achan "coveted" [chamad] the devoted things and then "took" them; Deut 7:25, where Israel is told not to "covet" [tachmod] the silver and gold on idols and "take it" for themselves). The Exodus version uses chamad twice (for house, then for wife and possessions), while the Deuteronomy 5:21 parallel uses chamad for the wife and then switches to hit'avveh ("to desire, crave") for the remaining items — a distinction suggesting that Deuteronomy differentiates between coveting a person and craving property. The formal equivalence rendering preserves the single verb "covet" for both clauses in the Exodus text. The LXX renders it ouk epithumēseis ("you shall not desire/covet"), using epithumeō — the verb that will appear in Romans 7:7 when Paul quotes this very commandment.

**ed shaqer** (v. 16) — "a witness of falsehood." The Exodus text reads ed shaqer ("a false witness," literally "a witness of deception/falsehood"), while Deuteronomy 5:20 reads ed shav ("a vain/empty witness"). The term shaqer denotes deliberate deception and lying, while shav carries connotations of emptiness, worthlessness, and unreliability (the same word used in the Third Commandment, v. 7). The distinction suggests that Exodus emphasizes the active falsehood of perjury, while Deuteronomy emphasizes its insubstantiality and worthlessness. The formal rendering preserves "falsehood" (shaqer) for Exodus.

**arafel** (v. 21) — "thick darkness." The noun arafel denotes the densest form of darkness — darker than choshekh ("darkness"), heavier than anan ("cloud"). It appears fifteen times in the Hebrew Bible, frequently in theophanic contexts (Deut 4:11; 5:22; 2 Sam 22:10; 1 Kgs 8:12; Ps 18:10; 97:2). Solomon will later declare: "The LORD has said He would dwell in the arafel" (1 Kgs 8:12). The thick darkness is not the absence of God but the superabundance of divine presence — God dwells in darkness because His glory is too intense for human sight. Moses, who alone can draw near to this arafel, occupies a position that no other Israelite can share.

### Grammatical Notes

**ro'im et-haqqolot** (v. 18) — "seeing the thunders." The Hebrew uses the participle of r-'-h ("to see") with qolot ("voices, sounds, thunders") as its object — the people are "seeing" what is normally heard. This synesthetic construction has generated extensive commentary. The Mekhilta (R. Akiva) takes it literally: they saw what is normally heard. The formal equivalence rendering preserves the synesthesia ("were seeing the thunders") rather than smoothing it to "perceived" or "witnessed." The LXX renders it hōra tēn phōnēn ("was seeing the voice"), preserving the same startling synesthesia in Greek.

**leva'avur nassot etkhem** (v. 20) — "in order to test you." The infinitive construct nassot (from n-s-h, "to test, prove, try") with the preposition leva'avur ("in order that, for the sake of") gives the purpose of the theophany. The "testing" is not a trap but a proving: God reveals Himself in terrifying majesty so that the fear of God will remain upon the people's faces and restrain them from sin. The fear produced by the theophany is therapeutic, not punitive — it is the means by which obedience becomes instinctive.

**henafta ... vatechalelha** (v. 25) — "you have swung ... and you have profaned it." The two perfects (henafta from n-w-f, "to swing, wave, wield"; vatechalelha from ch-l-l, "to profane, desecrate") establish causation: the mere act of wielding a tool upon the stones constitutes their profanation. The altar must be of unhewn stone — stone in its natural state, untouched by human craft. The prohibition links back to the garden: human technological intervention (the "sword," cherev, which is also the tool of the stonemason) introduces human agency into what must remain wholly God's. The altar's integrity depends on its freedom from human workmanship.

**lo ta'asun itti ... lo ta'asu lakhem** (v. 23) — "You shall not make alongside Me ... you shall not make for yourselves." The shift from itti ("with Me, alongside Me") to lakhem ("for yourselves") distinguishes two aspects of the prohibition: gods of silver must not stand alongside YHWH (as though He were one deity among many), and gods of gold must not be made for Israel's own use (as though Israel could fashion its own objects of worship). The two clauses cover both syncretism (other gods beside God) and idolatry (self-made gods for oneself).

### Structural Notes

Exodus 20 divides into three major sections: (1) the Decalogue proper, introduced by the divine preamble (vv. 1-17); (2) the people's fear and Moses' mediation (vv. 18-21); and (3) the altar law (vv. 22-26). This tripartite structure moves from divine speech directly to the people (vv. 1-17), through the people's refusal to hear God directly and their request for Moses as mediator (vv. 18-19), to mediated divine speech through Moses (vv. 22-26). The arc of the chapter is thus: God speaks to all Israel, Israel cannot bear it, and henceforth God will speak through Moses. The Decalogue is the last divine utterance heard by the entire nation without mediation.

The Decalogue itself follows an ancient Near Eastern treaty pattern: (a) the suzerain identifies himself (v. 2a: "I am the LORD your God"); (b) historical prologue establishing the suzerain's prior benefaction (v. 2b: "who brought you out of the land of Egypt"); (c) the stipulations (vv. 3-17). The commandments move from the vertical axis (duties toward God: vv. 3-11) to the horizontal axis (duties toward neighbor: vv. 12-17), with the Fifth Commandment ("Honor your father and your mother") serving as the hinge — it is the last commandment with a promise attached and the first that concerns a human relationship. The vertical commandments are expansive (multiple verses with motivation clauses), while the horizontal commandments are terse (single prohibitions without explanation), as though the relationship with God requires elaborate articulation while the duties toward neighbor are self-evident.

The numbering and division of the Ten Commandments varies across traditions. The Jewish tradition (following the Talmud) counts v. 2 ("I am the LORD your God") as the first "word" (dibbur) and combines vv. 3-6 as the second. The Reformed/Protestant tradition (following most ancient church fathers) treats v. 2 as preamble, v. 3 as the first commandment, and vv. 4-6 as the second. The Roman Catholic and Lutheran tradition combines vv. 3-6 into a single first commandment and divides v. 17 into two (coveting the wife as the ninth, coveting property as the tenth). This translation presents the text without prejudging the enumeration, using the textual divisions of the Masoretic text.

The altar law (vv. 22-26) is often treated as the beginning of the Covenant Code (which extends through Exod 23:33), but its placement immediately after the Decalogue and the theophanic response creates a theological continuity: the God who has just spoken the Ten Words now specifies how Israel is to approach Him in worship. The movement from commandment to worship law establishes the pattern that will govern all of Exodus 25-40: the ethical stipulations find their liturgical expression in proper worship. The prohibition against hewn stones (v. 25) and steps (v. 26) establishes a radical simplicity of worship — no human artistry, no elevation of the worshiper — that stands in deliberate contrast to the elaborate temple complexes of Egypt and Canaan.

### Theological Notes

The preamble (v. 2) is the hermeneutical key to the entire Decalogue. God does not introduce Himself as Creator ("I am the LORD who made heaven and earth") or as cosmic sovereign ("I am the LORD, ruler of all"), but as Redeemer ("I am the LORD your God who brought you out of the land of Egypt, out of the house of bondage"). The commandments are addressed to a people already redeemed, not to a people seeking redemption. Obedience is the response to grace, not the condition for it. The exodus precedes Sinai; liberation precedes law. Every "you shall not" rests on the prior "I brought you out." This ordering — indicative before imperative, gift before demand — is the deep grammar of biblical theology.

The phrase al-panay (v. 3, "before My face") has been interpreted variously: "before Me" (spatial — no other gods in My presence); "besides Me" (exclusive — no other gods in addition to Me); "in preference to Me" (hierarchical — no other gods ranked above Me); or "in defiance of Me" (confrontational — no other gods set against Me). The formal equivalence rendering "before My face" preserves the Hebrew's spatial metaphor and its multiple resonances. The phrase does not merely prohibit polytheism in the abstract; it prohibits the presence of other gods in the place where God's face is — that is, wherever God has made Himself known. Since God has just spoken from Sinai, and since He will dwell among Israel in the tabernacle, the prohibition effectively covers all of Israel's life.

The visitational formula in verse 5 — "visiting the iniquity of the fathers upon the sons, upon the third and upon the fourth generation" — is not a doctrine of inherited guilt but a description of sin's social consequences. The word poqed ("visiting") means to attend to, to reckon, to bring to account; it describes God's active engagement with the moral order. The limitation to three or four generations (the typical span of a living extended family in the ancient world) suggests that sin's consequences are bounded, while the contrasting "steadfast love to thousands" (v. 6) — whether thousands of generations or thousands of individuals — suggests that mercy vastly exceeds judgment. The ratio of judgment to mercy is 3-4 to thousands — a deliberate asymmetry.

The Sabbath commandment (vv. 8-11) grounds the day of rest in creation theology: God rested on the seventh day, therefore Israel shall rest. The Deuteronomy 5:15 parallel grounds the Sabbath in exodus theology: "Remember that you were a servant in the land of Egypt, and the LORD your God brought you out ... therefore the LORD your God commanded you to keep the Sabbath day." The two rationales are not contradictory but complementary: in Exodus, rest imitates God; in Deuteronomy, rest remembers liberation. The Sabbath is simultaneously a creation ordinance (participation in divine rest) and a freedom ordinance (refusal to be enslaved to unceasing labor). The inclusion of servants, animals, and sojourners in the Sabbath rest (v. 10) is revolutionary: rest is not a privilege of the free but a right of all living beings under Israelite jurisdiction.

The synesthesia of verse 18 — the people "seeing" the thunders — has been read as a description of theophanic perception that transcends normal sensory categories. At Sinai, the boundaries between sight and hearing collapse, just as the boundaries between heaven and earth, between the divine and the human, are being traversed by God's descent. The people's response — trembling, standing at a distance, and requesting mediation — is the appropriate human reaction to unmediated divine speech. The terror is not a failure of faith but the recognition of human finitude in the face of the holy.

Moses' statement in verse 20 contains a deliberate paradox: "Do not fear, for God has come in order to test you, and in order that His fear may be upon your faces." The people are told simultaneously not to fear and that the purpose of the theophany is to instill fear. The resolution lies in the two different Hebrew terms: the first "fear" (tira'u, from y-r-', in its negative sense of paralyzing terror) is what they should not do; the second "fear" (yir'ato, "the fear of Him," reverential awe) is what should permanently mark their lives. The theophany converts destructive panic into constructive reverence.

The altar law's prohibition against steps (v. 26) — "so that your nakedness may not be uncovered upon it" — links worship directly to bodily modesty and the covering of shame. The priests who later serve in the tabernacle will wear linen undergarments (Exod 28:42) precisely for this reason. The concern is not merely practical prudery but theological: the worshiper who approaches God must not expose what was covered after the fall (Gen 3:21, where God Himself made garments of skin). The altar law thus connects Sinai worship to Eden — the God who clothed Adam's nakedness will not have it uncovered at His altar.

---

*Note on the LXX (Vaticanus):* The Vaticanus text of Exodus 20 (pages 61-62) preserves the LXX version of the Decalogue with several significant divergences from the MT. At v. 2, the LXX renders anokhi YHWH Elohekha as ego eimi kurios ho theos sou ("I am the Lord your God"), replacing the emphatic anokhi with ego eimi — a self-identification formula that gains enormous Christological significance in the Fourth Gospel. At v. 3, al-panay becomes pro prosōpou mou ("before my face"), preserving the spatial metaphor of the Hebrew. At v. 4, the LXX renders fesel as eidōlon ("idol, image"), a word that will become the standard New Testament term for pagan idols, whereas the Hebrew fesel specifically denotes a carved or sculpted image. At v. 5, el qanna becomes theos zēlōtēs ("a zealous God"). The visitational formula is rendered as epi tritēn kai tetartēn genean ("upon the third and fourth generation"), making explicit what the Hebrew leaves implicit with the substantival adjectives shileshim and ribbe'im. At v. 7, lashshav becomes epi mataiō ("upon emptiness/vanity"), using mataios — the same word that dominates Ecclesiastes in the LXX (mataiōtēs mataiōtētōn, "vanity of vanities"). At v. 8, zakhor becomes mnēsthēti ("remember"), the aorist imperative. At v. 11, the creation rationale is preserved intact. At v. 13, the LXX renders lo tirtsach as ou phoneuseis, using the specific term for murder rather than the general term for killing. At v. 14, lo tin'af becomes ou moicheuseis. At v. 15, lo tignov becomes ou klepseis. Notably, Vaticanus preserves an order in the short commandments (vv. 13-15) that differs from the MT in some LXX manuscripts: some witnesses read "you shall not commit adultery, you shall not steal, you shall not murder" — though Vaticanus itself follows the MT sequence. At v. 16, ed shaqer becomes marturian pseudē ("false testimony"), replacing "witness" (ed) with "testimony" (marturia) — a shift from the person to the act. At v. 17, the LXX renders both instances of lo tachmod with ouk epithumēseis ("you shall not desire"), and notably separates the wife from the household goods with a distinct clause — ouk epithumēseis tēn gunaika tou plēsiou sou ("you shall not desire the wife of your neighbor") — giving the wife separate grammatical standing from the property list that follows. This separation anticipates the Deuteronomic division of the commandment into two verbs (chamad and hit'avveh). At v. 18, the synesthesia is preserved: ho laos hōra tēn phōnēn ("the people was seeing the voice"). At v. 21, arafel is rendered gnophos ("thick darkness, gloom"), a term Homer uses for the darkness of death and the LXX uses consistently for theophanic darkness. At v. 24, asher azkir et-shemi ("where I cause My name to be remembered") is rendered hou ean eponomasō to onoma mou ("wherever I name My name"), shifting from causative memory to divine naming — a significant theological move. At v. 25, the LXX renders charbkha as tēn smilēn sou ("your chisel"), specifying the mason's tool rather than the Hebrew's broader "sword/blade." Throughout the Vaticanus pages, the Decalogue text flows continuously in the three-column uncial format with no special spacing or ornamentation to mark the commandments — the text is treated as continuous divine speech, not as a numbered list.

--- LOGOS
